search_history.question: what is the full of nlp
search_history.answer: NLP stands for Natural Language Processing [Document: NLP-UNIT-1.pdf, Page: 3].

search_history.question: what is meant by unsmoothing n -grams
search_history.answer: An unsmoothed N-gram model calculates probabilities using only the text it has seen during training [Document: NLP-UNIT-2.pdf, Page: 5]. It uses Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE), which operates on the principle that if something happened many times before, it will likely happen again, and if it never happened, it is considered impossible [Document: NLP-UNIT-2.pdf, Page: 5]. A problem with unsmoothed N-grams is that unseen words get a probability of 0, which causes the sentence probability to become zero and perplexity to become infinite [Document: NLP-UNIT-2.pdf, Page: 11]. This is also referred to as the Sparse Data Problem, where most word combinations never appear, and the dataset does not contain enough examples for many word combinations [Document: NLP-UNIT-2.pdf, Page: 6].

search_history.question: what is are the types of ambiguity in nlp
search_history.answer: One type of ambiguity in NLP is POS Ambiguity, which is identified as a main challenge [Document: NLP-UNIT-2.pdf, Page: 37]. This occurs when one word can have many meanings, and the computer must choose the correct tag using the sentence [Document: NLP-UNIT-2.pdf, Page: 37]. Examples include the word "book" which can be a NOUN or a VERB, and "can" which can also be a NOUN or a VERB [Document: NLP-UNIT-2.pdf, Page: 37].

search_history.question: what are the challenges of nlp
search_history.answer: Understanding human language presents several challenges for NLP, including the fact that a word can have multiple meanings, sentence meaning is dependent on context, grammar can vary, people often mix languages, and language itself is constantly changing [Document: NLP-UNIT-1.pdf, Page: 8].

search_history.question: what are the challenges of nlp
search_history.answer: Understanding human language is difficult in NLP because a word can have more than one meaning, sentence meaning depends on context, grammar varies, people mix languages, and language keeps changing [Document: NLP-UNIT-1.pdf, Page: 8].
